sections.title: Networking basics
sections.subtitle: Connect, communicate, and transfer files across networks

lessons.title: Network interfaces — ip & ifconfig
lessons.description: View your network interfaces and IP addresses

steps.label: Viewing network interfaces
steps.text:
A <hl>network interface</hl> is how your computer connects to a network — physical (Ethernet, Wi-Fi) or virtual (loopback).

<cmd>ip addr</cmd>          — modern way to show all interfaces and IPs
<cmd>ip addr show eth0</cmd> — show a specific interface
<cmd>ifconfig</cmd>         — older tool (may need: sudo apt install net-tools)

Common interfaces:
<hl>eth0 / ens3</hl>  — wired Ethernet
<hl>wlan0</hl>        — Wi-Fi
<hl>lo</hl>           — loopback (127.0.0.1, always 'localhost')

Look for <hl>inet</hl> to find the IPv4 address and <hl>inet6</hl> for IPv6.

terminal.output:
2: eth0: <BROADCAST,MULTICAST,UP,LOWER_UP> mtu 1500
    link/ether 00:1a:2b:3c:4d:5e brd ff:ff:ff:ff:ff:ff
    inet 192.168.1.42/24 brd 192.168.1.255 scope global eth0
    inet6 fe80::21a:2bff:fe3c:4d5e/64 scope link

steps.label: Quick check
steps.question: What IP address is always assigned to the loopback interface (lo)?

feedback.ok: Correct! 127.0.0.1 (localhost) is always the loopback address — traffic to it never leaves your machine.
feedback.bad: 127.0.0.1 is always the loopback (localhost) address. It refers to your own machine.

steps.label: Modern vs legacy
steps.question: What is the modern replacement for the ifconfig command?

feedback.ok: Correct! ip (from the iproute2 package) is the modern tool. ifconfig is considered legacy.
feedback.bad: ip addr is the modern replacement for ifconfig. Most new Linux systems don't have ifconfig by default.

steps.label: Fill in the blank
sentence: In the output of ip addr, look for the
keyword to find your IPv4 address.

feedback.ok: Correct! Lines starting with 'inet' show IPv4 addresses. 'inet6' shows IPv6.
feedback.bad: 'inet' marks the IPv4 address in ip addr output. 'inet6' marks the IPv6 address.

steps.label: Write it yourself
steps.prompt: Show all network interfaces and their IP addresses.
steps.hint: Use ip addr (or the shorthand ip a) to list all interfaces.

output.ok:
<success>✓ Correct!</success>
<info>1: lo: <LOOPBACK,UP,LOWER_UP>
    inet 127.0.0.1/8 scope host lo
2: eth0: <BROADCAST,MULTICAST,UP,LOWER_UP>
    inet 192.168.1.42/24 brd 192.168.1.255 scope global eth0</info>
output.bad:
<e>✗ Not quite.</e>
<info>Try: ip addr (or ip a for short)</info>

lessons.title: Testing connectivity — ping
lessons.description: Test whether a host is reachable and measure response time

steps.label: Using ping
steps.text:
<cmd>ping</cmd> sends ICMP echo requests to a host and measures the round-trip time.

<cmd>ping google.com</cmd>       — ping until you press Ctrl+C
<cmd>ping -c 4 google.com</cmd>  — send exactly 4 packets then stop
<cmd>ping -i 2 google.com</cmd>  — wait 2 seconds between packets

What to read in the output:
<hl>time=12.5 ms</hl>  — round-trip time (lower = faster)
<hl>ttl=118</hl>       — time to live (hops remaining)
<hl>packet loss</hl>   — 0% is good, any loss = connectivity issue

<warn>Some servers block ICMP — a failed ping doesn't always mean the host is down.</warn>

terminal.output:
PING google.com (142.250.180.46) 56 bytes
64 bytes from 142.250.180.46: icmp_seq=1 ttl=118 time=14.2 ms
64 bytes from 142.250.180.46: icmp_seq=2 ttl=118 time=13.8 ms
64 bytes from 142.250.180.46: icmp_seq=3 ttl=118 time=14.1 ms
--- ping statistics ---
3 packets transmitted, 3 received, 0% packet loss

steps.label: Quick check
steps.question: You ping a server and see '100% packet loss'. What does this most likely mean?
options: The server is unreachable from your machine, or it blocks ICMP

feedback.ok: Correct! 100% packet loss means no response, but the server might still be up — some block ICMP pings.
feedback.bad: 100% packet loss means no response reached you. The server could be down, or it may be blocking ICMP traffic.

steps.label: Fill in the blank
sentence: To send only 5 ping packets and then stop automatically, use: ping

feedback.ok: Correct! -c sets the count. ping -c 5 google.com sends exactly 5 packets.
feedback.bad: -c sets the packet count: ping -c 5 google.com sends exactly 5 packets then exits.

steps.label: Reading output
steps.question: ping shows 'time=210 ms'. Is this good or bad for a local network?
options: High — local networks should show 1-5 ms
It depends on the server location

feedback.ok: Correct! Local network ping should be 1-5 ms. 210 ms suggests a problem with your local network.
feedback.bad: On a local network, ping times should be 1-5 ms. 210 ms is high and suggests network congestion or a problem.

steps.label: Write it yourself
steps.prompt: Ping 8.8.8.8 (Google's DNS server) sending exactly 3 packets.
steps.hint: Use ping -c 3 followed by the IP address.

output.ok:
<success>✓ Correct!</success>
<info>PING 8.8.8.8: 56 data bytes
64 bytes from 8.8.8.8: icmp_seq=0 ttl=118 time=12.4 ms
64 bytes from 8.8.8.8: icmp_seq=1 ttl=118 time=11.9 ms
64 bytes from 8.8.8.8: icmp_seq=2 ttl=118 time=12.1 ms
3 packets transmitted, 3 received, 0% packet loss</info>

lessons.title: Fetching data — curl & wget
lessons.description: Download files and make HTTP requests from the command line

steps.label: curl and wget
steps.text:
<hl>curl</hl> and <hl>wget</hl> both retrieve content from URLs, but have different strengths.

<cmd>curl https://example.com</cmd>              — print the response to stdout
<cmd>curl -o file.html https://example.com</cmd>  — save to a file
<cmd>curl -I https://example.com</cmd>            — show response headers only
<cmd>curl -X POST -d 'key=val' url</cmd>          — send a POST request

<cmd>wget https://example.com/file.zip</cmd>      — download a file (saves automatically)
<cmd>wget -q -O out.txt https://example.com</cmd> — quiet mode, save to out.txt

<hl>curl</hl> is better for API work and scripting. <hl>wget</hl> is simpler for just downloading files.

steps.label: Quick check
steps.question: You want to download a large file and have it automatically saved. Which tool is simpler for this?

feedback.ok: Correct! wget saves files automatically without extra flags. curl prints to stdout by default and needs -o to save.
feedback.bad: wget saves the file automatically. curl prints to stdout and needs -o filename to save to a file.

steps.label: Fill in the blank
sentence: To save a curl response to a file instead of printing it, use the

feedback.ok: Correct! curl -o filename.txt https://... saves the output to a file.
feedback.bad: -o tells curl where to save the output: curl -o output.txt https://example.com

steps.label: API testing
steps.question: Which curl flag shows only the HTTP response headers (not the body)?

feedback.ok: Correct! curl -I sends a HEAD request and shows only the headers.
feedback.bad: -I (uppercase) sends a HEAD request — you get the headers but not the response body.

steps.label: Reorder the command
steps.instruction: Download https://example.com/report.pdf and save it to report.pdf:

feedback.ok: Correct! curl -o output_file url is the standard format.
feedback.bad: Order: curl then -o flag then the filename then the URL.

steps.label: Write it yourself
steps.prompt: Use curl to print the contents of https://ifconfig.me/ip (shows your public IP) to the terminal.
steps.hint: Just use curl followed by the URL. No extra flags needed to print to the terminal.

lessons.title: Remote access — ssh
lessons.description: Connect securely to remote Linux machines

steps.label: Connecting with SSH
steps.text:
<hl>SSH</hl> (Secure Shell) lets you log in to remote Linux machines over an encrypted connection.

<cmd>ssh user@hostname</cmd>          — connect to a remote host
<cmd>ssh user@192.168.1.10</cmd>      — connect using IP
<cmd>ssh -p 2222 user@host</cmd>      — use a non-default port
<cmd>ssh -i ~/.ssh/mykey user@host</cmd> — use a specific key file

After connecting, you get a shell on the remote machine and can run commands as if you were sitting at it.

<cmd>exit</cmd> or <cmd>Ctrl+D</cmd>  — close the SSH session

SSH key authentication (more secure than passwords):
<cmd>ssh-keygen</cmd>         — generate a key pair
<cmd>ssh-copy-id user@host</cmd> — copy your public key to the server

terminal.output:
deploy@192.168.1.50's password:
Welcome to Ubuntu 22.04 LTS
deploy@server:~$

steps.label: Quick check
steps.question: What does SSH stand for and what does it protect?
options: System Shell Host — protects from viruses
Secure Shell — encrypts all traffic between you and the remote machine
Server Shell Host — provides faster connections

feedback.ok: Correct! SSH (Secure Shell) encrypts everything — commands, output, passwords, file transfers.
feedback.bad: SSH = Secure Shell. It encrypts the entire communication between your machine and the remote server.

steps.label: Fill in the blank
sentence: To connect as user 'admin' to the host 'server.example.com', run: ssh

feedback.ok: Correct! Format is ssh username@hostname.
feedback.bad: SSH format: ssh user@host. You'd use whatever username you have on the remote machine.

steps.label: SSH keys vs passwords
steps.question: Why is SSH key authentication preferred over password authentication?
options: Passwords are not supported by modern SSH
Keys are much harder to brute-force and can be used without typing a password each time
Keys allow multiple users to share one account

feedback.ok: Correct! SSH keys use public-key cryptography — nearly impossible to brute-force, and can be used with no password prompt.
feedback.bad: SSH keys are preferred because they're cryptographically strong (can't be brute-forced) and allow passwordless login when set up correctly.

steps.label: Reorder the command
steps.instruction: Connect to server 10.0.0.5 on port 2222 as user 'deploy':

feedback.ok: Correct! Flags come before the host: ssh -p 2222 user@host.
feedback.bad: Order: ssh then flags (-p 2222) then the destination (user@host).

steps.label: Write it yourself
steps.prompt: Connect to the remote server at 192.168.1.100 as user 'ubuntu'.

output.ok:
<success>✓ Correct!</success>
<info>ubuntu@192.168.1.100's password:
Welcome to Ubuntu 22.04.3 LTS
ubuntu@server:~$</info>

lessons.title: Transferring files — scp & rsync
lessons.description: Copy files securely between local and remote machines

steps.label: scp and rsync
steps.text:
<hl>scp</hl> (Secure Copy) copies files over SSH. <hl>rsync</hl> is smarter — it only transfers what has changed.

<cmd>scp file.txt user@host:/remote/path/</cmd>       — local to remote
<cmd>scp user@host:/remote/file.txt ./</cmd>           — remote to local
<cmd>scp -r folder/ user@host:/remote/</cmd>           — copy a directory

<cmd>rsync -av file.txt user@host:/path/</cmd>        — sync with verbose output
<cmd>rsync -av --delete src/ user@host:/dst/</cmd>    — mirror (delete remote files not in source)
<cmd>rsync -av user@host:/remote/ ./local/</cmd>      — pull from remote

rsync flags: <hl>-a</hl> = archive mode (preserve permissions, timestamps), <hl>-v</hl> = verbose, <hl>-z</hl> = compress during transfer

terminal.output:
sending incremental file list
index.html
style.css
sent 45.2K bytes  received 320 bytes  18.2K bytes/sec

steps.label: Quick check
steps.question: What is the main advantage of rsync over scp?
options: rsync only transfers files that have changed, making it much faster for repeated syncs
rsync can transfer to Windows machines

feedback.ok: Correct! rsync does delta transfers — only the changed parts of files are sent, saving bandwidth and time.
feedback.bad: rsync's key advantage is incremental transfer — it checks what's different and only sends the changes.

steps.label: Fill in the blank
sentence: To copy a directory recursively with scp, use the

feedback.ok: Correct! scp -r copies directories recursively, including all files and subdirectories.
feedback.bad: Use -r (recursive) with scp to copy entire directories: scp -r myfolder user@host:/path/

steps.label: rsync flags
steps.question: What does the -a flag do in rsync -av?
options: Archive mode: preserves permissions, timestamps, symlinks, and ownership

feedback.ok: Correct! -a (archive) is the recommended flag for most rsync jobs — it preserves file metadata.
feedback.bad: -a is 'archive mode' — it preserves permissions, timestamps, symlinks, and ownership. Almost always what you want.

steps.label: Write it yourself
steps.prompt: Copy the local file backup.tar.gz to /backups/ on the remote server at 10.0.0.5 as user 'admin'.

lessons.title: Checking connections — ss & netstat
lessons.description: See open ports and active network connections

steps.label: ss and netstat
steps.text:
<hl>ss</hl> (socket statistics) is the modern tool for viewing network connections. <hl>netstat</hl> is the legacy version.

<cmd>ss -tulnp</cmd>        — show all listening ports and which process owns them
<cmd>ss -an</cmd>           — show all connections (established + listening)
<cmd>netstat -tulnp</cmd>   — same as ss -tulnp (older systems)

Flags for ss/netstat:
<hl>-t</hl> = TCP, <hl>-u</hl> = UDP, <hl>-l</hl> = listening only
<hl>-n</hl> = show numbers not names, <hl>-p</hl> = show process name

Useful to check: is my web server actually listening? What's using port 3000?

terminal.output:
Netid  State   Recv-Q  Send-Q  Local Address:Port  Process
tcp    LISTEN  0       128     0.0.0.0:22         sshd
tcp    LISTEN  0       511     0.0.0.0:80         nginx
tcp    LISTEN  0       128     127.0.0.1:5432     postgres

steps.label: Quick check
steps.question: You run ss -tulnp and see port 22 in LISTEN state. What does this mean?
options: Port 22 is blocked by a firewall
SSH is running and accepting incoming connections

feedback.ok: Correct! LISTEN on port 22 means the SSH daemon is running and waiting for connections.
feedback.bad: LISTEN state means the process is bound to that port and accepting incoming connections. Port 22 is the default SSH port.

steps.label: Fill in the blank
sentence: The ss flags -tulnp stand for TCP, UDP, Listening, Numeric, and

feedback.ok: Correct! -p shows the process name and PID that owns each socket.
feedback.bad: -t=TCP, -u=UDP, -l=listening, -n=numeric, -p=process. The p flag shows which process owns each connection.

steps.label: Troubleshooting
steps.question: Your web app should be running on port 3000. You run ss -tulnp but don't see port 3000. What does this tell you?
options: The app is running but using a different protocol
The app process has crashed or never started properly
Port 3000 is reserved by the OS
You need to use netstat instead

feedback.ok: Correct! If the port isn't in the listening list, the app isn't running or failed to bind to that port.
feedback.bad: If the port doesn't appear in ss output, nothing is listening on it — meaning the process has crashed or wasn't started.

steps.label: Write it yourself
steps.prompt: Show all TCP ports currently in a LISTEN state along with which process is using them.

output.ok:
<success>✓ Correct!</success>
<info>Netid  State   Local Address:Port  Process
tcp    LISTEN  0.0.0.0:22          sshd (pid=812)
tcp    LISTEN  0.0.0.0:80          nginx (pid=1024)
tcp    LISTEN  127.0.0.1:3306      mysqld (pid=1102)</info>

steps.label: Review
steps.question: Which command shows all your machine's IP addresses on modern Linux?

feedback.ok: Correct! ip addr is the modern tool. ifconfig is legacy and may not be installed.
feedback.bad: ip addr (or ip a) is the modern way to list network interfaces and addresses.

steps.label: Review
steps.question: You want to download a file to a specific path using curl. Which flag do you need?

feedback.ok: Correct! -o filename tells curl where to save the response.
feedback.bad: -o sets the output filename: curl -o local_file.zip https://example.com/file.zip

steps.label: Review
sentence: To copy a local file to a remote server over SSH, use

feedback.ok: Correct! scp and rsync both transfer files securely over SSH.
feedback.bad: scp (Secure Copy) or rsync both transfer files over SSH. cp only works on local files.

steps.label: Review
steps.question: You run ss -tulnp and see port 80 is not listed. Your nginx config says it should be running. What's the problem?
options: Port 80 is reserved by the OS
nginx hasn't started or crashed — it's not listening
You need to run ss as root

feedback.ok: Correct! If nginx were running, port 80 would appear in ss output. It's not running.
feedback.bad: If a service is running, its port appears in ss -tulnp output. No port 80 = nginx is not running.

steps.label: Review
steps.instruction: SSH into server 10.10.0.1 using port 2200 as user 'ubuntu':

feedback.ok: Correct! ssh -p port user@host.
feedback.bad: Order: ssh then flags (-p 2200) then destination (user@host).

steps.label: Review
steps.question: You're syncing a website directory to a server repeatedly. Which is better: scp -r or rsync -av?
options: rsync -av, because it only transfers changed files

feedback.ok: Correct! For repeated syncs, rsync is far better — it only sends what changed, saving bandwidth and time.
feedback.bad: rsync is better for repeated syncs because it does delta transfers — only changed files are sent. scp always copies everything.
